coredata.dc:title: Photophysical studies of graphene quantum dots - Pyrene-derivatized porphyrins conjugates when encapsulated within Pluronic F127 micelles
coredata.dc:creator: Managa, Muthumuni
Achadu, Ojodomo J.
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Pyrene-derivatized H2, GaCl, and Zn porphyrins were immobilized on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form (GQDs-H2TPrP, GQDs-GaClTPrP, and GQDs-ZnTPrP) conjugates through the π-π stacking interaction method followed by encapsulating into Pluronic F127 micelles to form (GQDs-H2TPrP + F127, GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127, and GQDs-ZnTPrP + F127). Spectroscopic evidence shows that the resultant conjugates were stable due to the strong π-π stacking interaction between the GQDs and the porphyrins. The fluorescence and singlet oxygen generating behaviour of the porphyrins were investigated following incorporation. GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127 showed highest values of the binding constant (Kb). The Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv) for GQDs-ZnTPrP + F127 were the highest compared to other porphyrins derivatives.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pyrene-derivatized porphyrins
Singlet oxygen quantum yield
Fluorescence quenching
Graphene quantum dots
Pluronic F127 micelles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Nanocomposites of GQDs and H2TPrP and GaClTPrP
Incorporation of porphyrins into Pluronic F127
Determination of quenching and binding constants
Determination of binding constant Kb and number of binding sites n

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electronic absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs. Excitation wavelength at 340 nm in DMF.

Electronic absorption spectra of (A) H2TPyP (a) and GQDs-H2TPyP (b), (B) GaClTPrP (a) and GQDs-H2TPyP (b), (C) H2TPyP + F127 (a) and GQDs-H2TPyP + F127 (b), (D) GaClTPyP + F127 and GQDs-GaClTPyP + F127 (b) and (E) ZnTPyP + F127 and GQDs-ZnTPyP + F127. (A) and (B) in DMF, (C)-(E) in water.
Normalised fluorescence emission spectra of (A) ZnTPrP and GQDs-ZnTPrP in DMF, (B) H2TPrP + F127 and GQDs-H2TPrP + F127 in water. Excitation wavelength = 400 nm.
Raman spectra of GQDs (a), GQDs-H2TPrP (b), and GQDs-GaClTPrP (c) showing the corresponding Raman shifts, respectively.
Dynamic light scattering of (A) GQDs, (B) GQDs-GaClTPrP, (C) H2TPrP + F127, (D) GaClTPrP + F127 and (E) ZnTPrP + F127. (A) and (B) in DMF, (C)–(E) in water.
Transmission electron micrographs of (A) GQDs, (B) GQDs-GaClTPrP, (C) GQDs-H2TPrP + F127, (D) GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127, and (E) GQDs-ZnTPrP + F127.
Fluorescence decay profile of GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127 as an example in water.

Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) H2TPrP + F127 and (B) GaClTPrP + F127 in the presence of 0 M–0.5 M of KI. Excitation wavelength = 490 nm. Insert (a) Stern-Volmer plot using equation (6). Insert (b) using modified Stern-Volmer using equation (7).

Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) H2TPrP + F127 and (B) GaClTPrP + F127in the presence of 0 M, 0.022–0.033 M (20–30% w/v) of F127. The starting porphyrins alone already had Pluronic F127, hence 0 M and after addition of more F127. The porphyrins were excited at 490 nm.
Representation of the interaction between GQDs and the pyrene-derivatized porphyrins.
Representation of the incorporation of GQDs-porphyrin into Pluronic F127.
The photophysical parameters for the porphyrins and their conjugates with GQDs in DMF.
The photophysical parameters for H2TPrP + F127, GaClTPrP + F127, and ZnTPrP + F127 as well as the porphyrin-GQDs conjugates derivatives in water.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are the sizes determined by DLS in brackets.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are exciting where GQDs absorb.

head.ce:title: Photophysical studies of graphene quantum dots - Pyrene-derivatized porphyrins conjugates when encapsulated within Pluronic F127 micelles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pyrene-derivatized H2, GaCl, and Zn porphyrins were immobilized on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form (GQDs-H2TPrP, GQDs-GaClTPrP, and GQDs-ZnTPrP) conjugates through the π-π stacking interaction method followed by encapsulating into Pluronic F127 micelles to form (GQDs-H2TPrP + F127, GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127, and GQDs-ZnTPrP + F127). Spectroscopic evidence shows that the resultant conjugates were stable due to the strong π-π stacking interaction between the GQDs and the porphyrins. The fluorescence and singlet oxygen generating behaviour of the porphyrins were investigated following incorporation. GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127 showed highest values of the binding constant (Kb). The Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv) for GQDs-ZnTPrP + F127 were the highest compared to other porphyrins derivatives.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials-based drug vehicles link nanotechnology with advanced drug delivery system. Their formulations mainly include polymeric micelles, nanoparticles, liposomes [1,2], as well as carbon based materials such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), [3]. GQDs possess excellent optical and electronic properties coupled with high photostability, aqueous solubility and bio-compatibility [4,5]. These properties have made GQDs attractive alternatives to semiconductor QDs for applications in fluorescence bio-imaging, sensing (biosensors and chemosensors), photocatalysis, drug delivery and as photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy (PDT) [6–9]. In this work, we study the photophysical behaviour of GQDs when non-covalently linked to porphyrins. The presence of delocalized π electrons facilitates the interaction of GQDs with a wide array of aromatic molecules (such as porphyrins) through non-covalent π-π stacking.

Porphyrins are well known as photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy (PDT) [10]. Hence combining the two PDT agents (porphyrins and GQDs) is expected to improve PDT through synergistic effect. The GQDs were non-covalently coordinated to pyrene-derivatized porphyrins via π-π stacking interaction, followed by encapsulation into Pluronic F127 triblock copolymer. GQDs have been conjugated to porphyrins for photocatalysis, fluorescence sensing and theoretical studies [11–13]. But no singlet oxygen properties have been reported for porphyrin-GQDs conjugates, hence are a subject of this work.

Pluronic F127 is a triblock copolymer which is composed of poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) and poly(propylene oxide) (PPO) with a PEO-PPO-PEO structure [14]. Pluronic triblock copolymer spontaneously forms micelles with an estimated diameter of 30–50 nm at concentrations equal to or above the critical micelle concentration (CMC) [15–17]. The PEO and PPO segments on the micelles act as hydrophilic corona and hydrophobic core, respectively [14,18]. Several Pluronics have been approved by Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for oral or intravenous administration because they are widely employed as solubilizers, emulsifiers or coating agents due to their biocompatibility and high drug loading ability [19–22]. Pluronic F127 triblock-copolymer is considered as one of the most studied biocompatible polymer nano-carriers for PDT photosensitizers in in vivo studies [19,22]. Pluronic F127 triblock-copolymer's biocompatibility and relatively small size help to prevent their micelles from being recognized by proteins and macrophages therefore allowing a greater circulation time [20]. Encapsulation of porphyrins into Pluronic micelles results in water solubility of the conjugate for porphyrins that are not water soluble [23–25]. Semiconductor CdTe/ZnS core/shell quantum dots have been encapsulated into carboxylated Pluronic F127 triblock polymeric micelle [26]. However, the encapsulation of the less toxic GQDs into Pluronic micelles has not been reported. This work reports for the first time on the encapsulation of conjugates of GQDs with porphyrins into F127 micelles. The pyrene moieties on the porphyrins (structure shown in Fig. 1 ) will assist in the immobilization of the complexes onto the GQDs due to the delocalized ππ electron structure of pyrene and the porphyrin ring.

Pluronic F127 (MW ∼12,600 g/mol), potassium iodide, anthracene- 9,10-bis-methylmalonate (ADMA), metal free tetrakis-(4-sulfonatophenyl) porphyrin (H2TSPP), and Zn tetraphenyl porphyrin (ZnTPP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Dichloromethane (DCM) and dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Merck. All other reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used as received.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized following procedures reported elsewhere [27]. The syntheses of metal free (H2TPrP) [28], zinc (ZnTPrP) [28], and GaCl (GaClTPrP) [23] 5,10,15,20-tetra(1-pyrenyl) porphyrin have been reported. The syntheses of the nanocomposites of GQDs and ZnTPrP have also been reported [13].

Ground state electronic absorption spectra were recorded at room temperature using on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer and a 1 cm pathlength cuvette. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a Varian Eclipse spectrofluorometer. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were done on a Malvern Zetasizer nanoseries, Nano-ZS90. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a Carl Zeiss Libra transmission electron microscope operating at 100 kV accelerating voltage. A Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064 nm Nd:YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector) was used to collect Raman data.

Singlet oxygen generation in DMF (in the absence of micelles) was quantified using the singlet oxygen luminescence method (SOLM). An ultrasensitive Germanium detector (Edinburgh Instruments, EI-P) combined with a 1000 nm long pass filter (Omega, 3RD 1000 CP) and a 1270 nm band pass filter (Omega, C1275, BP50) was used to detect singlet oxygen phosphorescence band at 1270 nm. These studies were done in the absence and presence of sodium azide (a physical quencher of singlet oxygen). Details have been provided before [29].

Photo-irradiations for singlet oxygen studies in water (in the presence of micelles) were done using a General Electric Quartz line lamp (300W). A water filter was used to filter off infrared radiations. This means irradiation encompassed both the Q and Soret bands. Light intensity was measured with a POWER MAX5100 (Molelectron detector incorporated) power meter and was found to be 9.43 × 1018 photons s−1 cm−2.

Fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF) were determined by comparative methods [30], equation (1).
(1)
Φ F
=
Φ F

where F and FStd are the areas under the fluorescence curves for sample and standard, respectively. A and AStd are the absorbances of the sample and the standard at the excitation wavelength, respectively. n and n
Std are the refractive indices of the solvent used for the sample and standard, respectively. ZnTPP in DMF was used as a standard (
Φ F
Std
= 0.033 [31]) when exciting where porphyrins absorb, quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Φ F = 0.52) [32] was employed where the GQDs absorb. The ΦF and fluorescence lifetimes are represented as ΦF(Porp) and τ (Porp) when exciting where porphyrins absorb, and ΦF(GQDs) and τ (GQDs) when exciting where GQDs absorb. GQDs fluorescence quantum yields upon coordination to porphyrins are given by equation (2)
(2)

(the fluorescence quantum yield of the GQDs alone) was used as the standard, F
(GQDs) is the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs alone and
F
c o n j u g a t e
is the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs coordinated to the porphyrins at the excitation of wavelength of 340 nm.

The singlet oxygen quantum yields in DMF (for porphyrins and GQDs-porphyrin conjugates in the absence of Pluronic F127) were evaluated using the singlet oxygen luminescence method and equation (3) [33]:
(3)

where, I(t) is the phosphorescence intensity of 1O2 at time t,
τ D
is the lifetime of 1O2 phosphorescence decay,
τ T
is the triplet state lifetime of the standard or sample and B is a coefficient involved in sensitizer concentration and singlet oxygen quantum yield. The singlet oxygen quantum yields (
Φ Δ

(4)

is the single oxygen quantum yield for the standard (ZnTPP in DMF (
Φ △

= 0.53) [34]), B and refer to coefficient involved in sensitizer concentration and singlet oxygen quantum yield for the sample and standard, respectively; while A and
A
Std
refer to the absorbance of the sample and standard, respectively at the excitation wavelength.

Singlet oxygen quantum yield values in water (for porphyrins and GQDs-porphyrin conjugates in Pluronic micelles) were determined under ambient conditions using the chemical method with ADMA as a singlet oxygen quencher in water, and equations described before [35]. Tetrakis-(4-sulfonatophenyl) porphyrin (H2TSPP) in water was employed as a standard (
Φ Δ
S t d
= 0.51) [36]. The results from the two methods: SOLM and chemical quencher method have been reported to be comparable [34]. The chemical method was employed in water due to the low fluorescence signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocomposites of GQDs and H2TPrP and GaClTPrP
ce:section.ce:para: The non-covalent coordination (π–π stacking) of GQDs to H2TPrP and GaClTPrP was achieved following a method employed before for the Co and Zn derivatives [13] but with slight modification. Briefly: 2 mg of GQDs was added to 0.5 mg each of H2TPrP (4.5 × 10−4 mol) and GaClTPrP (4.0 × 10−4 mol), in 5 mL of dry DMF. The resulting mixture was ultrasonicated for 2 h; followed by stirring in the dark for 48 h. Ethanol was used to repeatedly wash the products so as to dispel the excess uncoordinated porphyrins and GQDs by centrifuging, until the solution in the centrifuge tubes turned colourless. The porphyrin and GQDs are soluble in ethanol while the conjugates are not. The solid products were dried under vacuum overnight at 60 °C. Then, 10 mL of dry DMF was added to the resulting solid products followed by sonication for 10 min. The homogeneous dispersions were stable for several days. The nanocomposites are represented as GQDs-H2TPrP or GQDs-GaClTPrP. The nanocomposites for the reported Zn derivatives are represented as GQDs-ZnTPrP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of porphyrins into Pluronic F127
ce:section.ce:para: The incorporation of all porphyrins into micelles was carried out using the solid dispersion method which has been previous described in literature [26,37]. This method involves cosolubilization of copolymer with the porphyrin which is then followed by rotative evaporation as follows: the porphyrins: H2TPrP (8.99 × 10−3 M), GaClTPrP (8.2 × 10−3 M), and ZnTPrP (3.6 × 10−3 M); and the conjugates (0.05 mg/ml): GQDs-H2TPrP, GQDs-GaClTPrP, and GQDs-ZnTPrP, were each mixed with Pluronic F127 (20% w/V, 1.5 × 10−2 M) in 10 mL DCM. The mixtures were sonicated for 15 min at room temperature. The solvent was then removed using the rotary evaporator. The obtained solid products were left in a vacuum desiccator for 12 h and then hydrated and vigorously stirred (Dubnoff metabolic shaking) at 70 °C for 4 h. The solutions were transferred onto test tubes and left for 24 h undisturbed for precipitation of porphyrins which were not bound onto the polymer which were then filtered and the required product was dried. The conjugates are represented as: porphyrin + F127 or GQDs-porphyrin + F127 (where F127 represents Pluronic F127).

The porphyrins are only soluble in water following the formation of micelles. Hence the data obtained in the presence of Pluronic F127 cannot be compared with that without the Pluronic F127 due to differences in solvents.
The structures of the porphyrins employed are shown in Fig. 1.

A representation of the nanocomposites of GQDs and the porphyrins via π–π stacking is shown in Scheme 1 and that for the incorporation of the GQDs-porphyrin nanocomposites into Pluronic F127 in Scheme 2 .

The electronic absorption spectrum of the GQDs (Fig. 2 ) shows maximum absorption at 334 nm which has been associated with the π-π* transition of the sp2 carbon electrons of the graphene core of GQDs [38]. Fluorescence emission spectra shows the maximum emission intensity at 445 nm upon excitation at 340 nm.

The ground state electronic absorption spectra of porphyrins are characterized by an intense band called the Soret or B band at around 400 nm, Fig. 3 . The four Q-bands of the metal free porphyrins collapsed into two Q-bands for the metallated derivatives. In all cases (for porphyrins alone, conjugated to GQDs or in Pluronic F127 micelles), slight red shifts are observed for the metalated derivatives when compared to metal free porphyrin, Table 1 (in DMF) and 2 (in water). Introduction of heavy metals results in a degree of perturbation and electron delocalisation within the porphyrin macrocycle [39]. Absorptions occurring at 322 and 339 nm (in Fig. 3) are due to the pyrene substituents on the porphyrin ring. For GQDs-porphyrin conjugates, modification in the spectra below 400 nm is due to the presence of GQDs.

The Soret band was weak for GQDs-ZnTPrP + F127, but still clearly present. There is a red shift in the Soret bands in the presence of GQDs for GQDs-ZnTPrP + F127, GQDs-H2TPrP + F127 and GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127 compared to in the absence of GQDs in ZnTPrP + F127, H2TPrP + F127, and GaClTPrP + F127, Table 2 . In the absence of Pluronic F127, very small red shifts in the Soret band are observed for GQDs-H2TPrP compared to H2TPrP, and GQDs-GaClTPrP compared to GaClTPrP. Red-shifts of Soret bands are often observed for distorted porphyrins [40].

Fig. 4 shows the emission spectra of ZnTPrP and GQDs-ZnTPrP in DMF, and H2TPrP + F127 and GQDs-H2TPrP + F127 (in water), as examples. The spectra are typical [41] of porphyrins with two bands differing in intensity. The presence of GQDs slightly decreases the intensity of the low energy band of the porphyrins.

GQDs are known to exhibit the diagnostic Raman bands termed the G and D bands resulting from the E2g tangential vibrational mode of the sp2 bonded carbons and the disordered A1g breathing vibrational mode of the aromatic sp2 carbon rings, respectively. GQDs alone displayed the characteristic peaks at 1554 and 1385 cm−1 for the G and D bands, respectively (Fig. 5 (a)). Shifts to higher wavenumbers in the G band are observed in this work for GQDs-H2TPrP and GQDs-GaClTPrP, Fig. 5 (b and c). A significant shift in the D band was observed for GQDs-H2TPrP. Shifts in Raman peaks were also reported for GQDs-CoTPrP and GQDs-ZnTPrP [13]. Shifts in the D and G bands are an indication of new complex formation.

In order to ascertain the sizes of the nanoconjugates, dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out both in the absence and presence of micelles, Fig. 6 . It has been reported that micelles should be small enough to evade detection and destruction by the reticular endothelial system therefore increasing the efficiency and circulation in blood of the encapsulated drug; hence the determination of micelle size is crucial for this work [42].

In all cases (Tables 1 and 2), metal free conjugates have smaller sizes as compared to the corresponding metalated derivatives. It has been reported that central metals within the cavity of the porphyrins change the hydrophobicity of the molecule [24]. DLS results show that there was increase in the sizes of the GQDs nanoconjugates compared to GQDs alone (with or without Pluronic F127) which shows the formation of supramolecular assemblies of the GQDs and the porphyrins.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to determine the morphology and size distribution of the as-synthesized GQDs, Fig. 7 . The GQDs are monodispersed with sizes in the range of 2–6 nm (average size ∼ 4.5 nm) as reported before [13], which is slightly less than the DLS size. The sizes determined by DLS have been reported to be larger than those determined by other techniques since DLS results tend to be skewed toward larger particles [43].

The increase in size in the presence of porphyrins suggests aggregation. Porphyrins are known to show aggregation upon covalent and non-covalent interactions with nanoparticles, due to the interaction between porphyrins adsorbed onto adjacent nanoparticles. Aggregation by π-π stacking is common in porphyrins [44].

The TEM images in the presence of Pluronic F127 show “cargo hold” encapsulated with the GQDs-porphyrin molecules. The GQDs-porphyrins molecules can be seen clearly that they have been encapsulated into the pores of the polymer. Triblock copolymers such as Pluronic F127 reduce the self-aggregation and increase solubility of porphyrins. The triblock copolymer core of the micelles consists of the hydrophobic PPO blocks that are separated from the aqueous exterior by the shell of hydrated hydrophilic PEO chains. By itself the core represents a “cargo hold” for incorporation therapeutic or diagnostic drugs. This behaviour is also observed for porphrin + F127 in the absence of GQDs (images not shown).

The quenching of the GQDs emission by ZnTPrP has been reported [13], similar quenching was obtained for H2TPrP and GaClTPrP in this work in the absence and presence of Pluronic F127 (figure not shown). The quenching may be due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and other process that deactivate the excited state. Table 1 shows the decrease in ΦF(GQDs) and (τGQDs) (when exciting where GQDs absorb) due to FRET. The decreases in ΦF(GQDs) and (τGQDs) for GQDs in conjugation with porphyrins are also observed in the presence of micelles, Table 2.

FRET is a non-radiative energy transfer process which occurs between a photoexcited donor fluorophore upon absorption of higher energy photons and an acceptor molecule in close proximity to the donor fluorophore [45]. FRET efficiency (Eff) was determined experimentally from the fluorescence quantum yields of the donor in the absence (
Φ
F
(
G Q D s
)
) and presence (
Φ
F
(
G Q D s
)
c o n j u g a t e
) of the acceptor using equation (5) [45]:
(5)
E f f = 1 −
Φ
F
(
G Q D s
)
c o n j u g a t e
Φ
F
(
G Q D s
)

FRET efficiencies were high for conjugates carried out in DMF compared to in water (in the presence of micelles) Table 3 . The largest Eff values are observed for Zn derivatives, this could be related to the large size which suggests more porphyrins quenching the GQDs.

The effect of GQDs on the porphyrins was evaluated by calculating the fluorescence quantum yields ((ΦF(Porp)) and lifetimes (τ(Porp)) of the porphyrins (exciting where porphyrins absorb). There were slight increases in the fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF(Porp)) of the GQDs-ZnTPrP, GQDs-H2TPrP and GQDs-GaClTPrP compared to ZnTPrP, H2TPrP and GaClTPrP, respectively, Table 1, this is due to the fact GQDs and porphyrin absorption overlap. Hence exciting porphyrins will also excite GQDs resulting in increased ΦF(Porp) values. There is also a large increase in the fluorescence lifetimes (τ
F(Porp) ) of the conjugates (Table 1) corresponding to increase in ΦF(Porp). A typical fluorescence decay profile for of GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127 as an example is shown in Fig. 8 . Mono-exponential decay profiles were obtained. The increases in the fluorescence parameters (Φ
F(Porp) and τ
F(Porp) ) upon coordination to the GQDs could further be due to FRET. In the presence of micelles (Table 2) there are slight increases in ΦF(Porp) in the presence of GQDs, but the fluorescence lifetimes are shortened, suggesting quenching.

The Ф△ values for H2TPrP, GaClTPrP, and ZnTPrP in DMF increase in the presence of GQDs, Table 1. The Ф△ values for H2TPrP + F127, GaClTPrP + F127, and ZnTPrP + F127 were determined to be 0.30, 0.38, and 0.39, respectively in water (Table 2), while the values for GQDs-H2TPrP + F127, GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127, and GQDs-ZnTPrP + F127, in water were determined to be 0.33, 0.40, and 0.42, respectively, Table 2. Thus, there are only very slight increases in the Ф△ values for Pluronic F127 encapsulated porphyrins in the presence of GQDs. It has been reported in literature [46] that an increase in Ф△ values is expected when porphyrins are incorporated into Pluronic polymers. According to literature [47], the PEO segment of the polymer may hinder collision of the porphyrin with surrounding molecules thus obstructing idle losses of the triplet state energy therefore increasing Ф△

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of quenching and binding constants

ce:section.ce:para:
The relative locations of porphyrins in Pluronic F127 micellar system were carried out by fluorescence quenching. I− was employed as a quencher. The quenching of the fluorescence of H2TPrP + F127, and GaClTPrP + F127, ZnTPrP + F127, and their conjugates GQDs-H2TPrP + F127, GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127, GQDs-ZnTPrP + F127 in water using KI was carried out. Stern-Volmer constants (KSV) were calculated using equation (6):
(6)
F o
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
where Fo and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and in the presence of the quencher, respectively, [Q] is the concentration of the quencher. Iodide is the quencher in this case.

The Stern–Volmer plots showed nonlinear relationship. These types of plots can occur in the case of purely collisional quenching but with some of the fluorophores being less accessible than others [48,49]. The inserts (a) in Fig. 9 are a characteristic feature of two fluorophore populations. Some porphyrins are buried in the polymer interior and are relatively inaccessible to iodide while the others are on the polymer surface and are more accessible [48].

The deviation from linearity in the Stern–Volmer plots is accommodated by treating the data based on a modified Stern–Volmer equation by Sam Lehrer Eq. (7) which takes into account situations where two populations of fluorophores exist, with one being accessible to quencher and the other being buried and not accessible [50].
(7)
F o
Δ F
=
1
(
[ Q ]
f a
K
s v
)
+
1
f a
where F0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of quencher, ΔF is the observed decrease in the fluorescence and Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant of the accessible fluorophore. f a is the fluorescence of the fluorophore which is accessible to the quenchers (Q). Plots of
F o
Δ F
versus 1/[Q] with a Y-axis intercept of 1/fa are shown in Fig. 9A and B (inserts (b), using H2TPrP + F127 and GaClTPrP + F127 as examples, the rest of the plots are shown in Fig. S1 (supporting information).

A lower KSV indicates that dyes are located in the micelle core, resulting in less encounter between incorporated photosensitizer and iodine [51]. The Ksv values obtained for H2TPrP + F127 was 0.69, GaClTPrP + F127 was 0.85 and ZnTPrP + F127 was 1.17 M-1. Thus, ZnTPrP + F127 is located more on the hydrophilic outer core (and can interact with the quencher more) than the corresponding H2TPrP + F127 and GaClTPrP + F127, since the former have larger Ksv values. Generally metals in the centre of porphyrins changes the hydrophobicity of the molecule therefore resulting in the different locations for the porphyrins. The difference between GaClTPrP + F127 and ZnTPrP + F127 could be the presence of the axial ligand in the former which may affect the location. The same trend was also observed for the GQDs-porphyrins conjugates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of binding constant Kb and number of binding sites n
ce:section.ce:para:
The study of the binding interaction between pluronic F127 and the porphyrins (H2TPrP, GaClTPrP, ZnTPrP) and their conjugates (GQDs-H2TPrP, GQDs-GaClTPrP, GQDs-ZnTPrP in water was carried out employing standard spectrophotometric techniques using Scatchard Eq. (8) from which the apparent binding constant Kb and number of binding sites n on the F127 were determined [50]. Plots of log
[
F o
− F
]
[
F
-
F
∞
]
against log [F127] provided the values for n (from slope) and Kb (from intercept).
(8)
Log
[
F o
− F
]
[
F
-
F
∞
]
= log
K b
+ n log
[
F 127
]
where F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Pluronic F127 (represented as F127 in equation (8)), respectively. F∞ denotes fluorescence intensity when the fluorophores are completely saturated with Pluronic F127, [F127] is the total concentration of Pluronic F127.

The concentration of the fluorophore (porphyrin) was kept constant and that of the quencher (Pluronic F127) was varied, Fig. 10 (using H2TPrP + F127 and GaClTPrP + F127 as examples), the rest of the plots can be found in supporting information Fig. S2). Kb values for GQDs-H2TPrP + F127, GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127 and GQDs-ZnTPrP + F127 were determined to be 7.94 × 104 M−1, 1.25 × 105 M−1, and 1.02 × 105 M−1, respectively, these values are higher than in the absence of GQDs for H2TPrP + F127, GaClTPrP + F127, and ZnTPrP + F127, Table 2. The values which were obtained for metallated derivatives were higher as compared to metal free derivatives, due to the presence of the central metal. Generally the central metals are known to increase the binding affinity [24].

The association of the porphyrins with specific binding sites n of micellar systems is strongly modulated by the presence of the metal coordinated to the porphyrin ring [24]. The highest binding sited were determined for GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127 to be 3.39.

GQDs were non-covalently coordinated to metal free (H2TPrP), GaCl (GaClTPrP) and Zn (ZnTPrP) 5,10,15,20-tetra(1-pyrenyl) porphyrins and then encapsulated into Pluronic F127 micelles. Various spectroscopic techniques were employed to characterize the nanocomposites and the interaction mechanism leading to the quenching of the GQDs emission was elucidated via Förster resonance energy transfer studies. The photophysical and fluorescence quenching studies were carried in water. The binding constant Kb was high for GaClTPrP + F127 as well as for GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127, while the number of binding site n were higher for GQDs-GaClTPrP + F127. Fluorescence and singlet oxygen quantum yields improved for porphyrins in the presence of GQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST)/Nanotechnology (NIC) and National Research Foundation (NRF) of South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) and Rhodes University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Online data Online data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2017.09.031.
